references.title: Accurate, timely, and portable: Course-agnostic early prediction of student performance from LMS logs

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Being at Risk of Failing in a Course
extracted.Students: 1606 students from a Information Management School in Western Europe
extracted.Features: [Total Clicks, Clicks (%), Online Sessions, Clicks/Session, Clicks/day, Forum Clicks, Discussions Viewed, Forum Posts, Folder Clicks, Resources Viewed, URLs Viewed, Course Clicks, Assessments Started, Assignments Viewed, Assignments Submitted, Submissions (%), Total Time Online, Average duration of session, Largest Period of Inactivity, Days with 0 clicks, Days with no clicks (%), PercCourse_NLogin (N= 1, …, 10)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All data is obtained from LMS logs. Each row represents a student-course pairing. For each pairing, the authors first decide what date would represent specific course curation thresholds and create a model for each dataset. 

All features were normalized to range [0,1], authors also perform Feature Selection.Hyper-Parameters used were selected from literature.

Class imbalance is addressed via SMOTE, without generating better results.

LSTM uses a sequential representation of a single feature instead of the whole feature set.
extracted.Models: ZeroR, Naive Bayes, CART, Logistic Regression, MLP, SVM, kNN, Random Forest, AdaBoost, GBoost, ExtraTrees, LSTM
extracted.Comments:
Authors do a model for each course duration threshold. Selection of best algorithm is done by comparison of The AUC score.

Then, the classifier is evaluated in terms of comparison against a ZeroR and vs a version of the model trained using oversampling via SMOTE.

Finally, these models are also compared against a different model trained on a different feature set - a sequence of numbers of clicks. While the LSTM does not outright beat the traditional classifiers, it does achieve remarkably competitive performances despite being trained on a single feature.

Approach is also tested by training the same models on a second dataset. Results do not beat original author’s approach.
extracted.Limitations:
Exclusive use of LMS logs. 

LSTM approach does not consider additional features.

LSTM approach was not shown to generalize well to different datasets.

Dataset was collected during a Covid-Era so warrants generalization to the back to school context.

Under-explored Feature Extraction Strategies.

No Model Hyper-Parameter Tuning asidde from LSTM.

Selection of 25 equal-length timesteps for LSTM warrants justification.

annotations.text: EWS can identify both students at risk and excellent students. Study uses a year-long dataset and tests 2 types of representation in a course-agnostic manner using various stages of course completion:

Findings show that it is possible to obtain somewhat reliable models at 25% course completion. LSTM got worsde performances but its use may be benefical since the difference is smaller at earlier stages and, given that it only uses a single feature, may be more practical to obtain.
